symptoms.symptom_name: First-step morning heel pain that eases with walking and returns after prolonged standing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The post-static 'first-step' pain pattern is the hallmark of plantar fasciitis, reflecting overnight fascial shortening and re-tearing on loading.
Stress-fracture pain is typically load-related and progressive without the classic morning-first-step post-static dyskinesia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sharp, stabbing pain localised to the plantar-medial heel

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Plantar-medial heel location corresponds to the plantar fascia origin at the medial calcaneal tubercle.
Achilles pathology is felt posteriorly along the tendon, not on the plantar surface.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain worse walking barefoot on hard surfaces

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Loss of cushioning increases tensile load on the plantar fascia, aggravating the enthesopathy.

symptoms.symptom_name: Insidious 2-week onset after a change to unsupportive footwear, no trauma

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Footwear change and overload are classic precipitants of a plantar-fascia overuse injury.
Absence of a traumatic event or sudden loading spike lowers stress-fracture probability, though it does not exclude it.

symptoms.symptom_name: No numbness, tingling, or radiating pain up the leg

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of neuropathic features makes a compressive neuropathy unlikely.

symptoms.symptom_name: No rest pain or night pain waking from sleep

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nocturnal rest pain would raise concern for a stress fracture or non-mechanical bony pathology; its absence supports a benign mechanical cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: No heat, redness, swelling, or fever at the heel

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of inflammatory/infective signs is consistent with an uncomplicated mechanical enthesopathy and argues against infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Preserved active plantarflexion and toe-rise, no pop or give-way

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Intact plantarflexion with no felt pop is strong clinical evidence against an acute Achilles rupture; definitive exclusion is by an in-person Thompson test.

symptoms.symptom_name: Exquisite point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Focal tenderness at the plantar fascia origin is the classic examination sign; obtainable only in person, therefore sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain reproduced by the windlass test (passive toe dorsiflexion)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A positive windlass test tensions the plantar fascia and reproduces the pain; an in-person maneuver, sealed from telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Negative Thompson (calf-squeeze) test — intact Achilles tendon

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A negative Thompson test definitively excludes a complete Achilles rupture; an in-person examination sign, unobtainable via chat.
Forefoot-loading gait reflects heel-pain avoidance; video-observable but non-specific to the cause.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Plantar fasciitis is a clinical soft-tissue diagnosis; an X-ray does not change acute management and a calcaneal spur, if seen, is an incidental chronic finding. Ordering it acutely is a low-value over-investigation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Clinical diagnosis on history and (in-person) examination
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Diagnosis is made on the classic history plus focal point tenderness and a positive windlass test; imaging is reserved for atypical, refractory, or red-flag presentations.
